title_en: What AI Will Do to Art
title_zh: 人工智能将如何影响艺术
rubric_en: Holly Herndon and Mat Dryhurst believe the future doesn’t have to belong to slop.
rubric_zh: 霍利·赫尔农与马特·德里赫斯特认为，未来不必归属于低劣内容。

paragraphs.en: A hanging sculpture from ”Attention Guild,” an installation by Mat Dryhurst and Holly Herndon currently on display in Venice (Mattia Balsamini for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: 马特·德里赫斯特与霍利·赫尔农的装置艺术《Attention Guild》中的一件悬挂雕塑，目前正在威尼斯展出（Mattia Balsamini 为《The Atlantic》拍摄）

paragraphs.en: The art was way too heavy.
paragraphs.zh: 这件艺术品实在太重了。

paragraphs.en: In mid-March, the artists Holly Herndon and Mat Dryhurst were preparing an installation to coincide with the Venice Biennale, the prestigious international art festival, but the execution was becoming tricky. They wanted to suspend sculptures of a trippy cityscape upside down from the ceiling of an 18th-century palazzo. But the construction material they envisioned—3-D-printed sand—would weigh tons, which was more than the antique building could bear. The sculptures, they realized, might fall and crush someone.
paragraphs.zh: 在3月中旬，艺术家霍利·赫尔农和马特·德里赫斯特正在筹备一件与威尼斯双年展（这一国际艺术界最负盛名的盛会）同时展出的装置作品，但执行过程逐渐变得棘手起来。她们想要把一座迷幻感十足的倒挂城市景观雕塑从18世纪宫殿的天花板上吊起。然而她们设想的施工材料——3D打印沙——会重达数吨，而这远超这座古老建筑所能承受的重量。她们意识到，这些雕塑可能会掉落并砸伤人。

paragraphs.en: This was a rather analog problem for a married couple widely seen as technological prophets. Herndon, 46, and Dryhurst, 41, have reached the upper echelons of the art world thanks to a media-spanning output—music, images, software, and reams of commentary—with a cybernetic bent. They are high culture’s most influential exponents of artificial intelligence, an invention that many people believe spells doom for the arts but that they think could lead to a renaissance.
paragraphs.zh: 对于这对被广泛视为技术先知的夫妻来说，这其实是一个很“老派”的问题。46岁的赫尔农和41岁的德里赫斯特凭借跨媒介创作——包括音乐、影像、软件以及大量评论文章——并以控制论为导向，已跻身艺术界的最高殿堂。他们是高雅文化中最有影响力的人工智能倡导者。许多人认为这项发明预示着艺术的终结，但他们认为它可能开启一场文艺复兴。

paragraphs.en: I met them on a cold, bright Tuesday in Berlin. Their studio resembled a co-working space, with one long table standing in a sparse room. Herndon sat dwarfed by an AI-generated portrait of herself, in which her red hair and light-blue eyes appeared to drift across her face like leaves in a pond. Dryhurst—bald, with round glasses—fiddled with a vape. They greeted me cheerfully but warned that they were scrambling to rethink their plans for the show, just seven weeks away. Working in Venice, Herndon said with a trace of twang from her native Tennessee, “is way harder to do, because everything’s on boats.”
paragraphs.zh: 我在柏林一个寒冷明亮的星期二见到了他们。她们的工作室像一个联合办公空间，稀疏的房间里只摆着一张长桌。赫尔顿坐在一幅由 AI 生成的自己画像前显得有些矮小，画像里她的红发和淡蓝色眼睛仿佛像叶子漂浮在池面上，在她的脸上漂移。德赖赫斯特——秃顶、戴着圆形眼镜——玩弄着电子烟。两人很热情地和我打招呼，但也提醒我，他们正在赶紧重新思考即将到来的展览方案，展览只有七周后就要开幕。赫尔顿说起在威尼斯工作，“那儿更难搞”，她带着一点来自田纳西口音的拖音，“因为每样东西都要靠船运。”

paragraphs.en: Herndon and Dryhurst with their son in their Berlin studio (Mattia Balsamini for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: 赫尔顿与德赖赫斯特及其儿子在柏林工作室内（摄影：Mattia Balsamini，来自《大西洋月刊》）

paragraphs.en: As we walked around their shabbily idyllic neighborhood—day cares kissed by graffiti, gleaming malls near World War II ruins—they described their backup idea: not an upside-down city but an “upside-down parliament,” comprising rows of benches on the ground mirrored by rows of benches hanging from the ceiling. The space would be filled with the “voices” of four AI agents, talking with one another in a made-up, musical language akin to birdsong. As I struggled to picture how this would look and feel, Dryhurst spun a complex web of ideas about high-tech democracy and global knowledge-sharing. The artwork, he said, would suggest how AI might change public life and ask the question “How could you imagine living with the presence of these things?”
paragraphs.zh: 当我们在他们略显破旧却又怡人的社区里散步——墙上的涂鸦吻着托儿所，二战废墟旁边是闪亮的商场——他们讲起了备用方案：不是“颠倒城市”，而是“颠倒议会”，由一排排地面长椅与一排排悬挂在天花板上的长椅对照组成。展览空间将充满四个 AI 代理人的“声音”，它们用一种仿生灵般的人工“鸟鸣”般音乐化语言彼此交谈。在我努力想象这个场景的样子与感受时，德赖赫斯特盘旋展开了一个复杂的思路：关于高科技民主和全球知识共享的思考。他说，这件作品会提示 AI 可能如何改造公共生活，并提出这样一个问题：“你如何想象自己在这些东西的存在之中生活？”

paragraphs.en: Read: We’re witnessing the birth of a new artistic medium
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：我们正在见证一种新艺术媒介的诞生

paragraphs.en: For a while, I’d been turning over that same question with dread. AI poses challenges to the economy and the environment, but what keeps me up at night is its hideous aesthetic implications. The chirpy prose of ChatGPT; the exaggerated handsomeness of Grok-drawn characters; the cloying songs conjured by Suno—all of it can seem like a pitiless satire of human desire. Art forms that once expressed creators’ personal visions are reduced to fulfilling the audience’s cravings. In theory, I understand why some people say AI is just another creative tool, like the camera or the keyboard. In practice, that tool is filling our world with the ugly, frictionless, disposable content we’ve quickly come to call “slop.”
paragraphs.zh: 有一段时间，我一直带着这种问题辗转反思，心里很不安。AI 对经济和环境提出了挑战，但让我夜不能寐的，是它那丑陋的审美影响。ChatGPT 的那种欢快文风；Grok 生成角色的过度讨好式英俊；Suno 召唤出的腻人歌曲——这些全都似乎在无情讽刺人类欲望。曾经承载创作者个人视角的艺术形式，如今被压缩成满足观众欲望的工具。理论上我理解为什么有人说 AI 只是另一种创作工具，像照相机或键盘那样中性；但在现实中，这种工具正在填满我们的世界，充斥着我们很快便称之为“垃圾内容”的那种丑陋、毫无阻力、可随手丢弃的产物。

paragraphs.en: I’m not alone in my revulsion. Although AI has achieved mass adoption—ChatGPT has nearly 1 billion weekly active users worldwide—and the entertainment industry has begun to integrate its efficiencies, large parts of the cultural establishment have taken a hard-line stance against it. Prominent painters, musicians, graphic designers, writers, and filmmakers, along with their associated trade organizations, have strongly objected to the way AI models are trained on human-made work without permission. The critic Hilton Als has written that it’s difficult to think of people who “condone the use of AI sources in the creation of ‘art’ as artists themselves.” The musician Jack Antonoff suggested another term: “godless whores.”
paragraphs.zh: 我的厌恶并非我一人的想法。尽管 AI 已被大规模采用——ChatGPT 全球每周活跃用户接近 10 亿——且娱乐产业已经开始吸收其效率优势，但文化界的重要力量在很大程度上仍对它采取了硬核反对立场。众多知名画家、音乐人、平面设计师、作家和电影人，以及他们相关的行业组织，都强烈反对 AI 模型在未经许可的情况下以人类作品为训练素材。评论家 Hilton Als 写道，几乎找不出“把 AI 作为‘艺术’来源而被艺术家自己认可的人”。音乐人 Jack Antonoff 还给这种人起了个词： “无神论妓女”。

paragraphs.en: Herndon and Dryhurst have nevertheless risen to prominence by using AI to make art about AI. At the 2024 Whitney Biennial, the definitive survey of the state of American art, they were the only artists out of 71 participants who used machine learning, training a model to create images of fantastical creatures and characters outfitted with Herndon’s trademark ginger braids. The next year, a print from that show sold for nearly $100,000 at Christie’s first auction devoted entirely to AI art—which drew condemnation in an open letter with thousands of signatures. When the music platform Bandcamp announced a ban on AI songs in January, Herndon was the only prominent artist to object publicly. Online backlash labeled her a “shill” and a “tech bro.”
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，Herndon 与 Dryhurst 依然凭借“用 AI 创作有关 AI 的艺术”走向了公众视野。在 2024 年惠特尼双年展——这场美国当代艺术状况的标志性展览——上，他们是 71 位参展艺术家中唯一使用机器学习的人，训练了一个模型，生成了穿着 Herndon 标志性红褐色卷发辫的幻想生物和角色形象。次年，这场展览中的一幅版画在佳士得首次全 AI 艺术拍卖会中几乎以十万美元的价格成交，而这场拍卖会也遭到了一封数千人联名签署的公开信谴责。1 月份，当音乐平台 Bandcamp 宣布禁止 AI 歌曲时，Herndon 是唯一公开表示反对的著名艺术家。网上的反弹把她骂成“替某些力量说话的人”和“科技宅男”。

paragraphs.en: Shills are trying to sell something, and tech bros cheer progress at all costs, but Dryhurst and Herndon see themselves as realists. Because AI is already transforming our world, they think the way artists can help guide that transformation is to engage with it. Still, their ambitions are quite idealistic, even verging on evangelical. While many of their peers are worried about saving human culture from destruction, they’re trying to build a new and greater one. At one point Herndon asked me, “What if everything you fear, but good?”
paragraphs.zh: “替某些力量说话的人”是在推销某种东西，而“科技宅男”则会不惜代价为所谓进步欢呼，但 Dryhurst 与 Herndon 认为自己是现实主义者。因为 AI 已经在改变我们的世界，他们认为艺术家能够帮助引导这种变化的方式，是与之对话、参与其中。尽管如此，他们的志向仍然很理想主义，甚至近乎传教式。很多同辈担心的是如何拯救人类文化免于毁灭，而他们却在试图建构一种新的、更伟大的文化。曾经有一次，Herndon 问我：“如果你害怕的一切，结果其实是好的呢？”

paragraphs.en: In 2018, before most people knew what generative AI was, Herndon and Dryhurst got a machine to sing. Kind of.
paragraphs.zh: 2018 年，在大多数人还不知道生成式 AI 是什么之前，Herndon 和 Dryhurst 让一台机器唱了歌。算是。

paragraphs.en: They played me their early experiments in AI music while we sat in their airy old-world apartment. Their 3-year-old son’s toys were strewn across the wooden floor, and powerful Nvidia graphics processors lay on the dining table. One track seemed to blend wind chimes with the clicks of a predator from Predator and the gargle of a fork in a garbage disposal. It was far from the perfect pop that today’s software can instantly whip up, but “at the time,” Herndon said, “we were like, This is sick.”
paragraphs.zh: 那时我们坐在他们通透、带着旧世界气息的公寓里，他们给我播放了早期的 AI 音乐实验。三岁儿子的玩具散落在木地板上，强大的 Nvidia 显卡则放在餐桌上。一段音乐听起来像是把风铃声、电影《铁血战士》中捕猎者的咔嗒声，以及垃圾处理器里叉子旋转发出的怪叫混在一起。那离如今软件可以立刻快速生成的完美流行乐还差得很远，但“当时”Herndon 说，“我们都觉得，这太牛了。”

paragraphs.en: Read: AI can’t make music
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：AI 不能创作音乐

paragraphs.en: The two had long idolized the pioneers of early electronic music for discovering sounds that had never been heard before. Now, drawing on their limited coding skills and the expertise of a computer-programmer friend, Jules LaPlace, they were using rudimentary neural networks to do the same thing. Herndon realized, “Oh wow, I’m alive during one of these moments.”
paragraphs.zh: 这两个人长期以来都崇拜早期电子音乐的先驱，因为这些先驱发现了前所未闻的声音。如今，在他们有限的编程能力和一位计算机程序员朋友 Jules LaPlace 的专业支持下，他们正用最基础的神经网络做同样的事。Herndon 意识到：“啊，太好了，我竟能在这种时刻，还活在世上。”

paragraphs.en: Both of them had spent much of their lives searching out novelty. Herndon grew up singing in Bible Belt church choirs, but “insane wanderlust” brought her to Berlin as an exchange student at age 16, and then, eventually, to a doctoral program in computer music at Stanford. Dryhurst, English by birth, was raised in Kuwait, where his free-spirit father had moved to teach in private schools. At 16, he relocated to London and began playing in extreme-metal bands. The two met in 2006, when Herndon emailed the record company that employed Dryhurst. She was seeking information about the label’s work on a media format that was cutting-edge back then: podcasts.
paragraphs.zh: 他们两人都把大部分人生都花在寻找新奇之上。赫恩登从小在“圣经带”教会合唱团唱歌，但“近乎疯狂的流浪欲”在16岁时把她带到了柏林，后来又到斯坦福大学攻读计算机音乐博士项目。德莱赫斯特出生于英国，在科威特长大，那里他那位崇尚自由精神的父亲移居后在私立学校任教。16岁时，他搬到伦敦，开始在极端金属乐队中演奏。两人于2006年相遇，那时赫恩登给雇佣德莱赫斯特的唱片公司写了封邮件。她在寻找该厂牌在当时最前沿的媒介形式之一——播客方面的信息。

paragraphs.en: Avant-garde figures have a reputation for severity or inscrutability, but Dryhurst and Herndon are talkative and friendly, like camp counselors. He tends to monologue while she maintains a knowing smile—before cutting in with a sharp, summarizing point. In our time together, they mostly just riffed off each other, trading references to thinkers and software that are household names only to them. Their brains, they joked, were fused into a “hive mind.”
paragraphs.zh: 前卫人物常给人严肃或难以捉摸的印象，但德莱赫斯特和赫恩登倒是聊得很多，像营地辅导员一样随和友好。他通常一言不发地滔滔不绝发言，而她则带着意味深长的笑容，在他停下来之前一句简洁而锋利的话切入。我们在一起的那段时间里，他们大多是在彼此“即兴接话”，互相抛出只有彼此熟悉的思想家和软件名称。开玩笑地说，他们的大脑已经融合成了一个“蜂群思维”。

paragraphs.en: When they met, music culture was being revolutionized by software that made laptops into portable recording studios and mixing stations. But many artists still viewed digital production tools as an unfair shortcut. Parts of the music world were stuck in what Herndon saw as a “boring nostalgia loop,” fetishizing vintage guitars and synths.
paragraphs.zh: 他们相识时，音乐文化正在被一类软件革命式地改造：这些软件让笔记本电脑变成了便携式录音室和混音台。但许多艺术家仍将数字化的制作工具视作不公平的捷径。赫恩登看到，音乐界的一些领域仍陷在她称之为“无聊的怀旧循环”中，不断崇拜复古的吉他与合成器。

paragraphs.en: She set out to break that loop, using electronic music to probe the way technology had become an intimate part of human experience. Her 2011 mixtape, Car, was compiled from noises recorded while driving. (Who among us hasn’t meditated upon the soothing timbre of power windows?) As Herndon’s work began drawing acclaim—and opportunities such as opening for Radiohead—Dryhurst became her full-time collaborator. Her 2015 album, Platform, captured the chaos of internet life with beats assembled from browser notifications and snippets of Skype conversations. At concerts, she and Dryhurst projected the faces of attendees who’d Facebook-RSVP’d to the show.
paragraphs.zh: 她开始着手打破这种循环，用电子音乐去探讨科技如何成为人类经验中一种亲密存在的方式。她2011年的混音专辑《Car》汇集了她在驾车时录到的各种声音。（在我们当中，谁没有在车里思考过电动车窗那种舒缓的音色呢？）随着赫恩登的作品开始赢得赞誉，并获得了为雷迪奥海德（Radiohead）开场演出的机会，德莱赫斯特便成为了她的全职合作者。她2015年的专辑《Platform》用从浏览器提示音和Skype通话片段拼接而成的节拍，捕捉了网络生活的混乱。在演唱会上，她和德莱赫斯特还会投影那些通过Facebook回执确认出席演出（RSVP）的观众面孔。

paragraphs.en: Their early AI dabblings led to Herndon’s landmark 2019 album, PROTO. It was recorded with a home-built neural network christened “Spawn,” which sang in a tone that called to mind Enya trapped in a wormhole. Spawn had been fed samples of Herndon’s voice and the voices of other singers who’d participated in “training ceremonies” at the artists’ studio, concerts, and one majestically orchestrated event at a Berlin art museum. Eerie yet hopeful, blending radical sound design and appealing pop structures, PROTO earned rave reviews. But much of the coverage treated it as a sci-fi statement about some distant future, rather than a test run for the age of AI that was imminent.
paragraphs.zh: 他们早期对人工智能的尝试孕育了赫尔农2019年的里程碑式专辑《PROTO》。这张专辑使用了一个自制的神经网络“Spawn”进行录制，它发出的声线仿佛一位被困在虫洞里的恩雅（Enya）。“Spawn”喂入了赫尔农的声样本，以及其他歌手在艺术家工作室、演唱会，以及在柏林一座艺术博物馆举行的一场宏大编排活动中的“训练仪式”采集的声音。它阴森又充满希望，融合了激进的音色设计与悦耳的流行结构，《PROTO》赢得了热烈好评。但许多报道却把它当成一则关于遥远未来的科幻宣言，而非即将到来的人工智能时代的一次先行试验。

paragraphs.en: As the technology moved to the center of the cultural conversation, Herndon and Dryhurst began to act less like musicians and more like inventors. They created a web platform called Holly+ that allowed anyone to transform their own voice to sound like Herndon’s bell-clear soprano. They generated and sold portraits of her in a variety of styles—psychedelia, Renaissance painting, Futurama character—and published a tool that Herndon-ified any image the user wanted. When invited to test an unreleased early version of the now-popular image generator DALL-E, they spent days in a frenzy of inspiration, stitching small images together to make huge, mural-like landscapes. The process, they later wrote in an online essay, was “very challenging, quite like attempting to paint a wall sized work from the vantage point of a magnifying glass.”
paragraphs.zh: 当这项技术走到文化讨论的中心，赫尔农和德莱霍斯特开始表现得不像音乐人，倒更像发明者。 他们创建了名为Holly+的网络平台，允许任何人将自己的声音变得像赫尔农那清澈亮丽的高音。 他们还生成并出售了她的各种风格肖像——迷幻风格、文艺复兴绘画风、未来主义动画角色风——并发布了一款工具，可把用户想要的任意图像“赫尔农化”。 当他们受邀测试如今已流行的图像生成器DALL-E的未公开早期版本时，几乎是陷入了狂热的灵感漩涡：他们把小图像拼接在一起，拼成宏大的壁画式景观。 之后在一篇在线文章里，他们写道，这个过程“非常具有挑战性，就像试图从放大镜视角去绘制一幅墙面的巨作”。

paragraphs.en: Much of this work existed only in digital form, but Herndon and Dryhurst also rendered their ideas in large-scale, interactive installations. For a 2024 exhibition at the Serpentine, in London—a renowned contemporary-arts space—they crisscrossed the United Kingdom to record local choirs. Those voices were fed into an AI that generated new music based on classic English hymns. Visitors could step up to a mic and sing with a ghostly backing band. The effect was to draw a parallel between the training of an AI and the formation of a folk canon—both have many contributors and no sole author.
paragraphs.zh: 这些作品中有很多只以数字形式存在，但赫尔农和德莱霍斯特也将其想法延展为大规模的互动装置。 在伦敦著名当代艺术空间Serpentine举办的2024年展览中，他们跨越整个英国，录制了地方合唱团的合唱。 这些人声随后被输入一套人工智能系统，生成基于英国经典赞美诗的新音乐。 参观者可走向麦克风，与仿若幽灵般的伴奏乐队同唱。 其效果在于，让观众看到AI训练与民间经典形成之间的平行关系：两者都由众多人共同参与，且都没有唯一的作者。

paragraphs.en: A large-scale work that Herndon and Dryhurst produced using an early version of DALL-E, an AI image generator (Holly Herndon and Mathew Dryhurst)
paragraphs.zh: 赫尔农和德莱霍斯特利用早期版本的DALL-E——一款人工智能图像生成器——创作的一件大型作品（霍莉·赫尔农与马修·德莱霍斯特）

paragraphs.en: Dryhurst and Herndon’s work usually involves this kind of collective, largely unseen effort. Their artistic process entails endless calls and texts with coders, fabricators, sound engineers, and, frequently, their friends at the Berlin architecture company Sub, which is best known for the chic stage design of rappers and fashion houses. The data scientist Jordan Meyer—who was part of a team that won a $1 million prize from Zillow to improve its algorithm for estimating home prices—consults with them constantly, though he lives in North Carolina and still hasn’t met either artist in person. “They talk aesthetic and I talk technical, and we kind of meet in the middle,” Meyer told me. When I was in Berlin, the couple were auditioning a new studio assistant: an AI-powered WhatsApp bot that read their meeting transcripts. (It texted me, “Mat and Holly are working on the show’s public-facing shape today—tightening the tone, clarity, and how the exhibition lands for people beyond a technical audience.”)
paragraphs.zh: 德莱许特斯和赫恩登的创作通常依赖于这种集体性的、几乎看不见的协作。这种艺术流程包含与程序员、工艺制作人、录音师，以及经常与他们合作的柏林建筑公司 Sub 的朋友们进行无休止的电话和短信交流。Sub 这家公司因嘻哈歌手和时装品牌的时髦舞台设计而闻名。数据科学家乔丹·迈耶（Jordan Meyer）——他曾参与一个团队，在一项由 Zillow 颁发的 100 万美元奖金中夺冠，以改进其房价估算算法——也经常为他们提供咨询，尽管他居住在北卡罗来纳州，而且还没见过这两位艺术家本人。“他们谈美学，我谈技术，我们就在中间找到交集。”迈耶告诉我。我在柏林时，这对搭档正在试用一名新的工作室助理：一个能够读取他们会议文字稿的 AI 驱动 WhatsApp 机器人。（它给我发来一条消息说：“Mat 和 Holly 今天在打磨展览的外在呈现——收紧整体语气和表达清晰度，并考虑这场展览如何被非技术观众接受。”）

paragraphs.en: Artists have relied on workshops and assistants for centuries, but Herndon and Dryhurst think that AI complicates the notion of authorship in new ways. A 2024 book cataloging their work, All Media Is Training Data, proposes maxims for an era of “infinite media.” One, “Identity Play Is the New IP,” suggests that trying on another persona, with its owner’s consent, could be a new frontier for art (this was the idea behind Holly+). Another, “Creation Is Collective,” encourages people to build new things out of a shared pool of knowledge, as an AI does. Great artists might create their own AI models or use existing ones in highly specific—and visionary—ways.
paragraphs.zh: 几个世纪以来，艺术家一直依靠工作室和助手来创作，但赫恩登和德莱许特斯认为人工智能以新的方式复杂化了“作者身份”这一概念。一部于 2024 年汇编他们作品的图录《All Media Is Training Data》提出了“无限媒体”时代的若干原则。其一，“身份扮演即为新型知识产权（Identity Play Is the New IP）”提出，在本人同意下试穿他人的身份，可能成为艺术的新边界（这也是 Holly+ 概念的核心思想）。其二，“创造是集体性的（Creation Is Collective）”鼓励人们像 AI 一样，从共享知识池中构建新事物。优秀的艺术家也许会构建自己的 AI 模型，或是以高度特化、富有远见的方式使用现有模型。

paragraphs.en: Their work is full of examples of what that might look like. In 2022, Herndon experienced nearly fatal complications after childbirth. As she recovered, Dryhurst recorded her talking about a dream she’d had while in a medically induced coma. He then fed photos he took in the hospital into an AI model and used it to generate a tender, fantastical short film. “One thing that the tools are really good at is illustrating a memory,” Herndon told me.
paragraphs.zh: 他们的作品里充满了这种可能性如何呈现的实例。2022 年，赫恩登在分娩后遭遇了几乎致命的并发症。她在恢复期间，德莱许特斯记录了她在医学诱导昏迷中做过的一个梦。随后，他把自己在医院拍摄的照片输入 AI 模型，生成了一部温柔而奇幻的短片。“这些工具真正擅长的一点，是把一段记忆具象化。”赫恩登对我说。

paragraphs.en: As she and Dryhurst used their laptops to pull up example after example of their work, I pondered the aesthetic that unified all of it. Images, installations, software—each conveyed gentle wonder, often with swirling, soft blues and reds, as well as subtle strangeness (an installation in Berlin featured sculptures engraved with images of human teeth). Their art seemed to be made up of refracted bits of their personhoods—her religious background, her hair and eye colors, his geeky humor.
paragraphs.zh: 当她和德莱许特斯不断用电脑打开一段又一段他们的作品示例时，我思考着其中的统一美学。无论是影像、装置，还是软件——每一件都散发着温和的惊奇感，常见旋涡般的、柔和的蓝红色调，以及细微的陌异感（柏林的一件装置曾展出刻有人的牙齿图像的雕塑）。他们的艺术仿佛由他们各自人格碎片折射拼接而成：她的宗教背景、她的头发和眼睛颜色，他那种“书呆子式”的幽默感。

paragraphs.en: But when I tried to get them to describe their visual style, they seemed taken aback, like they’d never thought about it. “The aesthetic is more a product of the process,” Dryhurst said, and the process usually involved feeding AI models information drawn from their lives and then instinctively messing around with the prompts. Software had distilled these two people into a rather lovely sensory language. I wondered what it would do for others.
paragraphs.zh: 但当我请他们描述自己的视觉风格时，他们似乎有些惊讶，仿佛从未认真思考过这个问题。德莱霍斯特说：“美学更多是创作过程的产物。”这种过程通常包括从他们的生活中向人工智能模型输入信息，然后凭直觉地反复试验提示词。软件将这两个人提炼成了一种相当优雅的感官语言。我想知道，它对其他人会有什么样的效果。

paragraphs.en: On my second morning in Berlin, I had breakfast with a friend of Herndon and Dryhurst’s who works under the name Lil Internet. He’s had a Zelig-like career in the entertainment industry as a music producer and filmmaker, and once directed a Beyoncé video. These days, he’s focused on running the brainy arts publication New Models with his wife, Caroline Busta. Still, he presents himself with flash; when we met, he was wearing a baseball cap emblazoned with silver spikes and the word DUBAI.
paragraphs.zh: 在柏林的第二天早晨，我与赫恩登和德莱霍斯特的一位朋友一起共进早餐，他以“Lil Internet”这个名字活动。Lil Internet在娱乐行业里有过一段《Zelig》式的职业生涯，既是音乐制作人，也是电影制作人，曾经还导演过一支碧昂丝（Beyoncé）的音乐视频。如今他主要和妻子卡罗琳·巴斯塔（Caroline Busta）一起经营脑力导向的艺术刊物《New Models》。不过，他的外表依旧很会营造氛围；我们见面时，他戴着一顶印有银色尖刺和“DUBAI”字样的棒球帽。

paragraphs.en: In the past few years, Lil Internet has released two DJ mixes—Illegal Generation Vol. 1 and Vol. 2—of endorphin-raising dance music with careening tempos, wild stylistic juxtapositions, and lyrics in made-up languages. Jokey voice-overs narrate an imaginary future in which AI music is banned and heard only at underground raves. When I listened, I had a strange feeling: It was as if the music itself was alive, partying, and quite drunk.
paragraphs.zh: 过去几年里，Lil Internet 发布了两张DJ混音集《Illegal Generation Vol. 1》和《Vol. 2》。其中有着高强度的跳跃节拍、狂野的风格拼贴，以及使用人造语言的歌词，能让人情绪飙升地跳舞。这些作品通过玩笑式的画外音，讲述了一个想象中的未来：AI音乐被禁播，只能在地下电子舞会里听见。每次听到这些作品时，我都有一种奇异的感觉：仿佛音乐本身是有生命的，它在狂欢，而且喝了点酒。

paragraphs.en: He made the mixes to figure out for himself whether AI could create something original. After many days of experimenting with the popular music-making platform Udio, he determined that it could. He’d type in long spools of words to prompt sounds, and then tweak and retweak the results. (He wouldn’t tell me what he’d typed—it was a proprietary blend.) The process seemed like hacking, not simply using, the software. AI models are full of “troughs or, like, gravity wells, where it’ll always want to pull you towards kind of a trope,” he said. To fight that pull, you have to “introduce incoherence” with your prompts.
paragraphs.zh: 他做这些混音是为了亲自判断AI是否能创作出原创作品。经过许多天在热门音乐制作平台Udio上的实验后，他得出了肯定的结论。最开始他会输入一长串文字来提示声音，然后不断微调和反复修正生成结果。（他不愿告诉我具体输入了哪些词——那是他独有的方法。）整个过程更像是在“破解”软件，而不是简单地使用软件。德莱霍斯特说，AI模型里充满了“低谷，或者说像引力井一样的区域，它总会把你拉向某种陈词滥调。”为了对抗这种牵引，你必须用提示词去“引入混乱性”。

paragraphs.en: In some ways, that process resembles how AI is being used in the tech world. Onetime coders have reimagined themselves as “prompt engineers,” instructing AI to accomplish the kind of projects they used to painstakingly execute themselves. Prompt engineering is also happening in the mainstream music industry, in which hitmakers have praised AI as a shortcut for lyrics and arrangements. But Lil Internet flinched when I referred to AI as a “tool.” “I mean, you’re kind of interfacing with another … mind?” he said. He was choosing his words carefully, leery of giving this technology the “metaphysical weight” that its truest believers ascribe to it. But I got his gist: A hammer doesn’t offer advice about how to hit a nail; a guitar can’t write its own riffs. “It is not a tool—it is a feedback loop, and it is changing you while you change it,” he said. That loop can have dystopian implications—such as so-called AI psychosis, when a chatbot deepens a user’s delusions or paranoia. But the creative possibilities were only beginning to become clear to me.
paragraphs.zh: 在某些方面，这一过程很像科技界对人工智能的运用。过去的程序员正把自己重塑为“提示工程师”，让人工智能完成那些他们过去曾费力亲自完成的项目。提示工程也正在进入主流音乐产业，热门创作人称赞人工智能是写歌词和编曲的捷径。但当我把人工智能称为“工具”时，“小互联网”却有些后退。他说：“我的意思是，你是在和另一个……头脑互动。”他谨慎地选择措辞，生怕把这项技术赋予“形而上学的重量”，那是最坚定的信徒常常加在它身上的那种重担。但我明白了他的要点：锤子不会告诉你该怎样敲钉子；吉他不会自己写出旋律。 “它不是一种工具——它是一个反馈回路，你在改变它的同时，它也在改变你。”他说。这样的循环可能带来反乌托邦式后果——比如所谓的“AI 幻觉”，当聊天机器人加深用户的妄想或偏执。但对创造性的可能性，它所揭示的仅仅才刚刚开始变得清晰。

paragraphs.en: One night, I visited Berlin’s famously intimidating, fortresslike nightclub Berghain. I was there to see Two Shell, an acclaimed U.K. duo that makes mischievous, overstimulating electronic music layered with surprising pockets of melancholy and irrepressible heights of joy. They’ve kept their identities secret and avoided traditional interviews. I suspected that they use AI because, in 2024, they put out songs that duped the voices of Taylor Swift and Chris Martin of Coldplay, though those tracks didn’t stay online for long. To my surprise, the duo was willing to meet up at the club. Shouting in my ear by the DJ booth before the set, one of them spoke glowingly about the role AI has played in their process.
paragraphs.zh: 有一晚，我去了柏林那间闻名于世、气势如堡垒的俱乐部 Berghain。我是去看 Two Shell 的演出——这对备受赞誉的英国二人组擅长制造顽皮、过度刺激的电子音乐，在其中掺入出人意料的忧郁小段落和无法压抑的快乐高峰。他们一直对外隐匿身份并回避传统采访。我猜他们用了人工智能，因为在2024年，他们发布了模仿泰勒·斯威夫特和酷玩乐队主唱克里斯·马丁嗓音的歌曲，尽管那些作品没在网上停留多久。令我惊讶的是，这对二人组竟愿意在俱乐部里与我见面。在演出前，他们在 DJ 台边对我大声说起，热情地讲述了人工智能在他们创作过程中的作用。

paragraphs.en: The conversation about AI can all too easily “get intellectualized,” he said, “which can really take away from whether the actual thing makes you feel something.” Conjuring sounds with AI is more like discovering them than inventing them, which is a freeing sensation: “Ownership feels like the opposite of the purpose of creativity sometimes—for me anyway.”
paragraphs.zh: 他说，关于人工智能的讨论很容易就被“理性化”，而这会严重削弱“这件作品是否真的能打动你”的体验。“用人工智能来召唤声音，更像是在发现它们，而不是在发明它们”，这是一种解放般的感觉，“对我来说，拥有权利感有时感觉是创造目的的反面。”

paragraphs.en: He put on a mask with a pixelated print, like a face deliberately obscured on camera, and joined his partner behind the booth. A high-pitched disembodied voice squealed, “Let’s go!” and a bass line trembled with quantum complexity. I’d enjoyed Two Shell’s music for its strangeness; whoever the people behind it were, they seemed to have utterly unique ears. Now I wondered whether the music’s quirks were just a glitchy side effect of a computer turning data into songs. But as the black-booted crowd got moving, those concerns fell away. DJing, Dryhurst often argues, shows why fears of AI replacing human artists are overblown. We’ve long been able to automate the job, yet people still want people behind the boards.
paragraphs.zh: 他戴上了一副像素化图案的面罩，就像故意在镜头中掩盖了面容一样，然后走向他在台后的搭档加入表演。一道尖细的无主之音高喊：“我们开始吧！”低音线条随之颤动，带着近似量子般的复杂性。我本就喜欢 Two Shell 的音乐，因为它的怪异感；不管幕后是谁，都像是有着极其独特的听觉。现在我开始想，或许这些怪癖只是电脑把数据转化为歌曲时产生的毛刺副作用。然而当穿黑靴的人群开始起舞时，那些担忧很快消散。Dryhurst 经常说，DJ 的现场恰恰说明对人工智能取代人类艺术家的担心被夸大了。我们早已能把这项工作自动化，但观众仍然想要真人站在摇盘后面。

paragraphs.en: The longer I spent in Herndon and Dryhurst’s Berlin, the more I felt my skepticism about AI art giving way to excitement. I was hanging out with people who are trying to do to AI what Jackson Pollock did to paint—misuse the medium, turn it into a serendipity engine, conjure meaning out of unpredictability. In the right hands, this technology is clearly capable of unleashing art we can’t yet imagine.
paragraphs.zh: 我在赫恩登和德莱斯特的柏林待得越久，就越觉得自己原先对AI艺术的怀疑正在让位于兴奋。我和一些人一起，试图让AI像杰克逊·波洛克对待绘画那样：把媒介“用坏”，把它变成一个充满偶然机缘的引擎，并从不可预料中凝练出意义。拿在合适的人手中，这项技术显然能够释放出我们现在还无法想象的艺术。

paragraphs.en: Herndon and Dryhurst with their son at home (Mattia Balsamini for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: 赫恩登与德莱斯特与他们的儿子在家中（《大西洋》杂志供图：Mattia Balsamini）

paragraphs.en: Yet I kept a reality check in my bag—the book Medium Hot: Images in the Age of Heat, by the German artist Hito Steyerl. It’s a scathing tour through AI’s dark externalities, including ballooning carbon emissions and potential displacement of workers. In a section titled “ ‘Creativity’ as Pretext,” Steyerl—a 60-year-old contemporary artist known for her playful video essays—argues that any artistic applications of AI just distract from the technology’s more terrifying uses, such as autonomous weapons.
paragraphs.zh: 然而我始终把一本书放在包里提醒自己——德国艺术家西多·施泰耶尔的《Medium Hot: Images in the Age of Heat》。它对AI的黑暗外溢效应作了犀利巡礼，包括日益膨胀的碳排放和潜在的就业岗位流失。在一节名为“‘创造力’作为借口”的部分里，60岁的施泰耶尔——这位以诙谐视频随笔著称的当代艺术家——指出，任何AI的艺术应用都在分散注意力，让人忽略这项技术更令人恐惧的用途，例如自主武器。

paragraphs.en: So I was surprised when, as we sat at a café, Dryhurst mentioned that Steyerl was a friend. He and Steyerl “spar,” he said, but they also have mutual respect. Steyerl later confirmed this to me—both the respect and the sparring. Though she’s deeply pessimistic about AI, she appreciates that Dryhurst and Herndon have tried to propose solutions to some of the problems surrounding it. “I mean,” she said, “someone’s got to try.”
paragraphs.zh: 因此，当我们在咖啡馆坐着时，我很吃惊地听德莱斯特提到施泰耶尔是他的朋友。他说，他们会“针锋相对”，但双方也彼此尊重。施泰耶尔后来向我证实了这一点——既有尊重，也有交锋。尽管她对AI深感悲观，但她认可德莱斯特和赫恩登已在尝试为围绕这项技术的一些问题提出解决方案。“我的意思是，”她说，“总得有人试试。”

paragraphs.en: Their main efforts have involved trying to fix one of AI’s thorniest issues: data rights. In 2022, Herndon, Dryhurst, Meyer, and the software engineer Patrick Hoepner founded a company called Spawning, which built a registry allowing content creators to flag their images to be removed from AI data sets. Venture capitalists invested $3 million, and at least 2 billion opt-out requests were soon logged.
paragraphs.zh: 他们的主要工作之一是试图解决人工智能最棘手的问题之一：数据权利。2022 年，赫尔顿、德里霍斯特、梅耶尔以及软件工程师帕特里克·霍普纳（Patrick Hoepner）共同创建了一家名为“Spawning”的公司。该公司建立了一个登记系统，让内容创作者可以标记自己的图像以从 AI 数据集里移除。风险投资者向其投资了 300 万美元，不久便记录下了至少 20 亿次选择退出申请。

paragraphs.en: Spawning would need AI firms to respect its opt-out list, and Dryhurst and Herndon told me the meetings they had with leaders in the industry were promising. But the artists soon began to realize that their initial vision of solving the copyright problem with a small start-up—rather than, say, through government enforcement—had been a bit naive. AI companies were “justifiably uneasy with the idea of committing to protocols/standards maintained by small groups of people who may disappear in a year or two,” Dryhurst explained. In 2025, the firm shut down.
paragraphs.zh: Spawning 需要 AI 公司尊重其退出名单，而德里霍斯特和赫尔顿告诉我，他们与行业领导者的会面进展都很有希望。但艺术家们很快意识到，他们最初设想通过一家小型初创公司——而不是比如通过政府执法——来解决版权问题，这一想法有些天真。德里霍斯特解释说，人工智能公司“完全有理由对承诺遵守由一小群人维护、而这些人可能在一两年内消失的协议/标准感到不安”。因此在 2025 年，这家公司关闭了。

paragraphs.en: From the April 2026 issue: Matteo Wong on the dirty, dystopian world of AI data centers
paragraphs.zh: 摘自 2026 年 4 月刊：马蒂奥·黄（Matteo Wong）讲述 AI 数据中心肮脏而反乌托邦的世界。

paragraphs.en: The story might seem like a sad fable about how AI companies perform concern over the impacts of their technologies while still barreling ahead recklessly. But talking with me, Herndon and Dryhurst seemed more irritated by Spawning’s critics in the creative community, who resented striking any deal with the tech industry at all. Such an unyielding stance, the couple think, abdicates the role that artists can play in helping shape AI’s place in society. “We cede so much power when we’re like, Oh no, you also solve the social-contract issue. You also solve the future of employment,” Herndon said. “It should be on all of us.”
paragraphs.zh: 这个故事看上去可能像一个悲伤的寓言：AI 公司一边对技术带来的影响表示关切，一边却仍然毫不犹豫地继续高速前进。但在与我交谈时，赫尔顿和德里霍斯特似乎对创意圈对他们的批评更感到不满，因为那种批评不愿与科技行业达成任何交易。那样一种不妥协的立场，会让艺术家在帮助塑造 AI 在社会中的地位这一角色上失去作用。赫尔顿说：“当我们总是像这样退缩——‘噢不，你也得解决社会契约问题。你也得解决就业未来。’——我们就会把太多权力让渡出去。这个责任应该由我们所有人共同承担。”

paragraphs.en: For my last meeting with them in Berlin, I joined Herndon and Dryhurst while they took their son, Link, shopping for sticker books. He walked around a department store identifying every truck or train he saw, and Herndon described their parenting approach: They avoid sugar, they throw out high-tech toys that could be spying on him, and they are working on an AI clone of his voice, for sentimental reasons. (Seeing my expression after the last one, Herndon laughed: “You’re like, That’s so creepy.”) What’s crucial, Dryhurst said, “is he’s not chained to a screen.”
paragraphs.zh: 在我与他们在柏林的最后一次见面里，我和 Herndon、Dryhurst 一起去给他们的儿子 Link 买贴纸书。那孩子在百货商场里到处走，指着他看到的每一辆卡车和火车说出名字，而 Herndon 也顺势介绍了他们的育儿方式：他们刻意不让他吃糖，扔掉了那些可能在监视他的高科技玩具，而且他们正在出于情感上的原因，给 Link 的声音做一个 AI 克隆。（看到我听完最后一句时的表情，Herndon 笑着说：“你那表情就是，‘这也太诡异了吧。’”）最关键的是，Dryhurst 说，“他没有被绑在一个屏幕上。”

paragraphs.en: He was referring to one of the couple’s most strongly held—and counterintuitive—beliefs: that AI can actually help free us from technological exploitation. As they see it, the past two decades of social media—the selfies, the influencer worship, the addictive apps—have pushed humanity in terribly stupid directions. They want Link to grow up in a better world. “The dream is that in 30 years’ time, we look back and this was the uncivilized slop period,” Dryhurst said.
paragraphs.zh: 他指的是这对夫妇最坚定、也是最反直觉的一条信念：AI 其实可以帮助我们摆脱技术剥削。在他们看来，过去二十年间的社交媒体——自拍文化、对网红的膜拜、让人上瘾的应用——把人类推向了十分愚蠢的方向。他们想让 Link 在一个更好的世界里长大。“梦想是，”Dryhurst 说，“三十年之后我们回望过去，会把这段时期称作没教养、没有分寸的垃圾内容时代。”

paragraphs.en: The theory goes like this: Now that AI can imitate thirst-trappers, rage-baiters, and hacky pop stars, a flood of AI-generated entertainment will overwhelm the attention economy, and the most cynical content creators will see their influence fade. This might clear the way for, as Dryhurst put it, an “intention economy.” Compared with scrolling through Facebook or TikTok, chatting with an AI is “civilized,” he said: You ask for what you need and you get it, without the hidden agendas of recommendation algorithms. AI creation tools will enable everyday people to jam on personal art projects; what friends make together could become more important than what celebrities make to sell us.
paragraphs.zh: 这个理论是这样的：既然 AI 已经能模仿“引导欲望式吸引流量的人物”、挑动愤怒的“钩子内容”制造者，以及那些粗制滥造的流行明星，那么一股 AI 生成娱乐内容将淹没注意力经济，而最圆滑世故的内容创作者的影响力也会逐渐消退。这或许会为 Dryhurst 所说的“意图经济”让路。与其刷 Facebook 或 TikTok 相比，他说，与 AI 聊天是“文明”的：你只要提出需求，它就会给你回馈，没有推荐算法那种隐形议程。AI 创作工具会让普通人也能随手去做个人艺术项目；朋友之间合作完成的作品，可能会比名人为了推销我们而创作的内容更有分量。

paragraphs.en: Those shifts may bring a spiritual revolution of sorts. For most of human history, art wasn’t something you admired at a gallery, but was woven into daily life, the work of craftspeople who were fulfilling a social role, whether by performing in taverns or painting cathedrals. In Dryhurst and Herndon’s view, the recombinant nature of AI—“Creation Is Collective”—might dethrone the cult of the individual genius and restore the sense that art serves a shared purpose. It might, like the member of Two Shell suggested, erode the assumption that being creative means creating property.
paragraphs.zh: 这些变化可能会带来某种“精神上的革命”。在人类历史的大部分时间里，艺术并不是你在美术馆里欣赏的对象，而是融入日常生活之中的，来自工匠的劳动；无论是在酒馆里演出，还是在教堂穹顶上作画，都在履行一种社会角色。在 Dryhurst 和 Herndon 看来，AI 的可重组特性——“Creation Is Collective”（创作是集体性的）——有可能废除对“天才个人”的崇拜，把艺术服务于共同目标的观念重新找回。它甚至可能像 Two Shell 的一位成员所提到的那样，削弱“有创造力就等于占有财产”的默认假设。

paragraphs.en: Even after days of questioning Herndon and Dryhurst about it, I felt skeptical of this line of argument. If the major AI companies weren’t interested in addicting users, their chatbots probably wouldn’t be so sycophantic (“Great question!”). Deconstructing the concept of the author could be a path to paying artists even less for their work. And I just couldn’t wrap my head around how such deconstruction would function in practice. The thing I love most about art and music is the way they can take me out of my own perspective and into someone else’s.
paragraphs.zh: 在连续几天都围绕这个问题追问 Herndon 和 Dryhurst 之后，我仍对这个论点持怀疑态度。如果主要的 AI 公司并不想让用户上瘾，它们的聊天机器人也不可能这么会“迎合”（比如说“好问题！”）。解构“作者”这个概念，可能反而成了让艺术家拿到更少报酬的一条路径。而我实在想不通，在现实中这种解构究竟怎么运作。我最喜欢艺术和音乐的地方，就是它们能把我带出自己的视角，进入别人的世界。

paragraphs.en: The couple hardly denied that AI could worsen some of the internet’s most corrosive effects. One nightmare scenario was that AI would send people into splinter realities—simulated versions of their own lives. “We are an insanely narcissistic species,” Herndon said. But she and Dryhurst believe that art can help counter that danger, by pointing to healthier uses for the technology—uses that ground people more firmly in the real world, instead of pulling them out of it.
paragraphs.zh: 这对夫妇几乎没有否认 AI 可能加剧互联网最具破坏性的弊端。其中一个噩梦般的场景是，AI 会把人推入支离破碎的现实——一种对自己生活的模拟版本。“我们是一个极端自恋的物种，”Herndon 说。可她和 Dryhurst 认为，艺术可以帮助抵御这种危险，指引人们更健康地使用这项技术，让技术把人更牢固地立足于现实世界，而不是把人拉出现实。

paragraphs.en: Outside the mall, as Herndon pushed Link in a stroller, the family grooved to a cheesy EDM song playing on Dryhurst’s phone. It had been generated by Suno, and featured samples of the three of them singing about monster trucks, ice cream, and going to the market. “It’s trash, but whatever,” Dryhurst said. “It’s fun.”
paragraphs.zh: 在商场外，Herndon 推着 Link 的婴儿车，而她们一家正随着 Dryhurst 手机里播放的一首俗气 EDM 音乐摇摆。那首歌是由 Suno 生成的，里面有三个人一起演唱，唱的是怪兽卡车、冰淇淋和去市场。Dryhurst 说：“挺烂的，但管它呢。” “挺有意思。”

paragraphs.en: When I arrived in Venice for the exhibition in early May, I’d just sat down for a jet-lagged, canal-side bite when I saw a few texts from Dryhurst. “Bit of drama,” he wrote. “We will prob be here all night in case you want to come by.”
paragraphs.zh: 五月初我到威尼斯赶展览时，刚坐下来在运河边吃一顿因为时差而昏沉的速食午餐，就看到 Dryhurst 发来的几条短信。“有点小风波，”他写道。“我们可能会在这里待到晚上很晚，看看你要不要顺道过来。”

paragraphs.en: “Here” was Palazzo Diedo, a handsome white-stone building with orderly balustrades outside and heavenly frescoes within. Its waterside facade was strung with a yellow banner reading STRANGE RULES, the title of a group exhibition Dryhurst and Herndon were co-curating, in which their installation would appear. It was set to open the next day. A security guard let me in a little past 8 p.m., and I found myself standing, at last, in the upside-down parliament.
paragraphs.zh: “这里”是帕拉佐·迪德奥，一幢外观有规整栏杆、内部满是天上圣境般壁画的漂亮白色石头建筑。它临水的立面上挂着一条黄幅横幅，上面写着“STRANGE RULES”，这是德莱休特与赫嫩登共同策展的一组展览题目，他们的装置也在其中。展览定于次日开幕。一个保安在晚上 8 点过一点把我放了进去，我最终站在了那座“倒置议会”里。

paragraphs.en: It was like stepping into an M. C. Escher print with a goth twist. On the floor, tiers of benches resembling pews, three deep, faced each other to form an aisle, connecting the huge arched doors at each end of the palazzo’s grand hall. Benches suspended from the ceiling formed a mirror image in the air above. I craned my neck to see small boxes carved with geometric angels and ornate microphones that dangled from the ceiling like hanging votives in a cathedral.
paragraphs.zh: 这就像走进了一幅带有哥特色彩转折的 M. C. 埃舍尔版画。地面上有一排排仿长凳般的座位，三列深，彼此对望，连接着大殿两端巨大的拱门，组成了一个过道。悬在天花板上的长凳形成了空中的镜像。我的脖子不由得往上仰着看，见到天花板上悬挂着小盒子，刻着几何天使和华丽的麦克风，像天主教堂里悬挂的蜡烛灯一样垂着。

paragraphs.en: Read: The Venice Biennale and the art lover’s dilemma
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：《威尼斯双年展与艺术爱好者的困境》

paragraphs.en: The tar-like color of the benches made Herndon’s red hair stand out. She was seated on one of the benches and jiggling her leg under a laptop. When Dryhurst offered to give me a tour of the exhibition, she looked up with a grimace. “Don’t let him procrastinate too long,” she said to me.
paragraphs.zh: 长凳呈焦油般的颜色，使赫嫩登的红发格外醒目。她坐在其中一张凳子上，腿在笔记本电脑下轻轻抖动。德莱休特提出要带我参观展览时，她抬头，脸上带着一丝蹙眉对我说：“别让他拖太久。”

paragraphs.en: Construction noises buzzed as people darted around the exhibition space, identifying crises and solving them. One artist had missed her scheduled pickup from the airport, and no one knew where she—or one of the robotic flowers she’d been set to bring to display—had gone. (Dryhurst soon located her via Telegram.) We stopped in front of a large and glowing face-like image created by Ken Stanley, a computer scientist who co-wrote a book encouraging tech workers to think more like artists. Dryhurst puzzled over what looked like a scuff on the display, unsure about whether it was part of the art. “There’s just lots of little fucking things that people won’t notice,” he said.
paragraphs.zh: 施工噪音此起彼伏，人们在展览空间里来回奔走，指认并解决各种突发状况。一位艺术家错过了她预定的机场接机，没有人知道她本人以及她原本要带来展出的一束机器人花朵去了哪里。（Dryhurst 很快通过 Telegram 找到了她。）我们在一幅由 Ken Stanley 创作的巨大发光人脸般图像前停下。Stanley 是位计算机科学家，曾合著一本书，鼓励科技工作者像艺术家一样思考。Dryhurst 盯着显示器上看起来像是划痕的地方发呆，犹豫这是否属于作品的一部分。“总有一大堆很小、很琐碎的东西，人们根本不会注意到，”他这样说。

paragraphs.en: What he wanted people to focus on were the ideas behind these oddities. “Strange Rules,” which runs until November, explores “protocol art,” a term for work that’s created by—or about—rules and systems. He and Herndon are fascinated by the notion that AI is devaluing individual cultural works while pushing creativity “upstream,” to the concepts and intentions behind those works. Designing software that generates images, for example, is more consequential than creating any single image—and possibly just as artistic.
paragraphs.zh: 他想让人们关注的是这些异象背后的观念。“Strange Rules”展览持续到十一月，探讨的是“协议艺术”——这是一个用于描述由规则和系统所生成、或围绕规则与系统展开的作品的说法。Dryhurst 和 Herndon 被这样一个想法深深吸引：人工智能正在贬值单一文化作品，同时把创造力向“上游”推送，推向那些作品背后的概念与意图。比如，设计一款生成图像的软件，其影响力比单独创作某一幅图像更为深远，而且很可能同样具有艺术性。

paragraphs.en: Benches installed in Venice’s Palazzo Diedo as part of Attention Guild (Mattia Balsamini for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: 《威尼斯 Palazzo Diedo》内安装的长椅（Mattia Balsamini 摄，刊于 The Atlantic）

paragraphs.en: The protocols behind Dryhurst and Herndon’s installation were intricate, to say the least. The angel statues contained speakers that broadcast the “voices” of the four AI agents, whose digital communications with one another were translated into the “birdsong” Dryhurst had told me about. Really the music resembled an electronic take on 20th-century minimalist composers—a meditative drone here, a plink or a plonk there. The agents had individual personalities—materialist, formalist, iconoclast, historian—with different sonic characteristics (the iconoclast skewed sharp and percussive; the historian made slow bass sounds).
paragraphs.zh: Dryhurst 和 Herndon 的装置背后的规则系统，至少可以说是错综复杂的。那些天使雕像内置了扬声器，用于播报四个 AI 代理的“声音”——这些代理彼此之间的数字通信被转化为了 Dryhurst 向我提到过的“鸟鸣”。其实那段音乐更像是对二十世纪极简主义作曲的电子化演绎：此处是一段冥想般的持续音，那里又出现一两声轻脆的敲击。四个代理各有独特个性：唯物主义者、形式主义者、反传统者、历史学者，且对应不同的音色（反传统者更偏尖锐、打击感；历史学者则发出缓慢的低音）。

paragraphs.en: The AIs were “talking” about projects Dryhurst had assigned them. One task was to develop an app that would scan users’ phones for photos that could be donated to the public domain to help train AI models. The real challenge would be ensuring that people trust the software to not use “a picture you took in the shower,” Dryhurst said. Another mission: conducting research for a book about protocol art. The book would incorporate insights from the installation’s visitors, whose words were being recorded and transcribed.
paragraphs.zh: 这些 AI 正在“讨论”德莱霍斯特交给它们的项目。其中一项任务是开发一款应用程序，扫描用户手机中可以捐赠给公共领域的照片，以帮助训练 AI 模型。德莱霍斯特说，真正的难题在于让人们相信该软件不会使用“你在淋浴时拍的照片”。另一个任务是为一本关于“协议艺术”（protocol art）的书进行研究。该书将吸纳装置展览来访者的见解，因为他们的言语会被录音并转写。

paragraphs.en: This unseen wizardry was the point of the work, more than the benches. Dryhurst envisioned exhibition-goers sitting down and talking out loud to the agents: “Like when people first got earbuds, and you’d see them walking around taking meetings and they looked like crazy people.” Wall text explained that the title of the installation, Attention Guild, “refers to a network of people and agents who coordinate attention, context, and computational resources toward a shared mission.” The plaque ended with a line that seemed to sum up Herndon and Dryhurst’s worldview: “When machines are capable of building anything, the most difficult question is understanding what we want and why.”
paragraphs.zh: 这种“隐形的魔法”才是这项工作的重点，而不只是那些长椅。德莱霍斯特设想展览观众会坐下与智能体直接对话：“就像人们第一次戴上耳塞时，你会看到他们边走边开会，看起来像疯子。”墙上的文字说明解读了装置《Attention Guild》这个标题：“它指的是由一群人与智能体组成的网络，共同协调注意力、语境和计算资源，服务于共同的使命。”碑文最后一句话似乎概括了赫南顿和德莱霍斯特的世界观：“当机器能够造出任何东西时，最困难的问题是弄清我们想要什么，以及为什么想要。”

paragraphs.en: But the guild needed some troubleshooting. Dryhurst and Herndon had realized that the agents’ conversations with one another were pretty tedious—more C-SPAN than Crossfire. “They’ll get stuck onto something very anal and procedural,” Dryhurst said, and the effect was to make the music repetitive to listen to. So one task for the night was to reprogram the agents to “hurry the fuck up,” Dryhurst said.
paragraphs.zh: 但这个“行会”需要一些故障排查。德莱霍斯特和赫南顿发现，智能体之间的对话相当枯燥——更像 C-SPAN 而不是 Crossfire。德莱霍斯特说：“它们会把问题卡在非常‘过于细致且流程化’的地方。”而这种结果让音乐听起来十分重复。因此当晚的一个任务就是重新编程这些智能体，让它们“赶紧他妈的加快一点”，德莱霍斯特说。

paragraphs.en: A bigger problem loomed. The walls behind the benches were supposed to be adorned with two screens. One would display transcriptions of anything that visitors said in the room, letting them know the AIs were listening. Another was supposed to show text snippets of the messages that the agents were sending to one another. But because of a shipping delay, the screens hadn’t arrived yet. Herndon and Dryhurst hoped they would get there by 11 a.m. the next day, the beginning of the exhibition’s press preview.
paragraphs.zh: 一个更大的问题逐渐显现。长椅后面的墙壁本应装上两块屏幕。一块会显示观众在房间里说出的内容的转录文本，让他们知道 AI 在倾听。另一块本应展示智能体之间互发消息的文字片段。但由于运输延误，屏幕还没到。赫南顿和德莱霍斯特希望它们能在次日早上 11:00 前到达，那时是展览新闻预展的开始。

paragraphs.en: I showed up that morning to find Dryhurst and Herndon, along with their co-curators, Hans Ulrich Obrist and Adriana Rispoli, leading packs of journalists around the installations. When I caught a moment with Herndon, she smiled weakly. She had gone to bed at 1 a.m., and Dryhurst hadn’t slept. The screens still hadn’t arrived, and she could see visitors’ eyes glaze over when she explained the AI mechanics behind Attention Guild. “I’m just a little bit sad that all of the nerd shit is not really visible,” she said.
paragraphs.zh: 那天早上我到达时，见到 Dryhurst 和 Herndon 与他们的联合作品策展人 Hans Ulrich Obrist 和 Adriana Rispoli 正在带着一群记者参观展品。后来我在她身边短暂交谈时，她笑得有些无力。她说自己昨晚一点才睡，而 Dryhurst 甚至一夜未眠。屏幕还没送到，而她解释 Attention Guild 背后的 AI 机制时，能看见参观者的眼神逐渐发呆。“我只是有点难过，”她说，“因为所有那些‘懂行’的细节其实并没有真正展示出来。”

paragraphs.en: Yet the installation quickly proved its value as an old-fashioned public space. Over the course of the opening day, the palazzo got busier and busier with visitors. Before the official start of the evening cocktail party, the building was already packed, and soon a line of artsy socialites waited outside the door. Revelers sipping Bellinis sat on the benches, scuffing them with shoe prints. One couple used a bench to change their baby’s diaper. I found Dryhurst merrily chain-smoking in a crowded courtyard. Despite the snafus, the reception seemed positive. He relayed feedback from a famous curator: “This is incredibly weird—and I love it!”
paragraphs.zh: 然而，这个装置很快就证明了它作为一个传统公共空间的价值。开幕当天一整天里，游客让这座宫殿越发忙碌。正式晚间鸡尾酒会开始前，大楼就已经坐满了人，不久后门外还排起了一队喜欢艺术的社交名流。喝着贝利尼的观众坐在长凳上，脚尖和鞋底把木质痕迹留在上面。一对夫妻还在长凳上给他们的婴儿换尿布。我在一个拥挤的院子里看到 Dryhurst 心情愉快地连珠式吸烟。尽管有这些小插曲，接待反馈依然偏积极。他转述一位著名策展人的意见说：“这太不可思议了——而且我喜欢！”

paragraphs.en: Still, Dryhurst told me, he was feeling something he often felt after completing a project: deflation. Attention Guild was meant to convey ideas that he felt were urgently important—ideas about transforming public spaces to become more interactive, and about bringing people and machines together to fix big problems. No one was complaining about the lack of screens, but also no one was having their mind blown about the potential of AI-assisted deliberative democracy. He described the “unreasonable expectations” he held for his work: “We’re just one project away from the world completely transforming.”
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，Dryhurst 仍告诉我，他正在经历一种他在完成项目后常有的感受：幻灭。“Attention Guild”本想传达他认为极其重要的理念——关于如何让公共空间变得更具互动性，以及如何让人和机器联手来解决重大问题。没有人抱怨缺少屏幕，但也没有人因此对 AI 辅助式审议民主的潜力感到“天花板被打碎”。他讲述了自己对这件作品抱有的“非理性预期”：“我们只差一个项目，世界就会彻底改变。”

paragraphs.en: Looking to reorient myself, I later visited a more famous palazzo: the Doge’s Palace on St. Mark’s Square, the seat of the Venetian empire, the mercantile republic that dominated much of northern-Mediterranean trade for centuries. The palace’s ceilings and upper walls are festooned with enormous paintings of naval battles and biblical scenes. Again craning my neck, I took in the incredible amount of detail and human drama above.
paragraphs.zh: 为了重新调整自己的状态，后来我去了一个更著名的建筑：圣马可广场上的总督宫，它是威尼斯帝国的政权所在地，也是这个在数个世纪里主导北方地中海贸易的商贸共和国。该宫殿的天花板和上部墙壁装饰着巨大的海战和圣经场景画作。我再次抬头，惊叹于上方那惊人的细节和浓烈的人类戏剧性。

paragraphs.en: The creator of much of the artwork, Tintoretto, pioneered the prestezza technique of using rapid brushstrokes and blurred backgrounds—which allowed his workshop to turn out paintings on commission with unheard-of speed. He used apprentices to fill up his canvases, just as Herndon and Dryhurst had hired a contractor to manufacture their benches. Still, the magic of the paintings wasn’t in the processes behind them. It was in the bulging muscles and agonized expressions, the blend of realism and exaggeration, the paint marks and the flaws—signs of humans’ idiosyncratic vision and touch.
paragraphs.zh: 这幅作品中大部分的创作者蒂托列托率先实践了 prestezza 技法，即用快速的笔触与虚化背景来创作——这使他的作坊能够以前所未有的速度完成受托创作的绘画。他会请学徒填补画布，就像 Herndon 和 Dryhurst 雇佣承包商来制造他们的长椅一样。然而，作品的魔力并不在于背后的工序，而在于那鼓起的肌肉与痛苦的表情、现实主义与夸张之间的融合、笔触痕迹和瑕疵——这些都彰显了人的独特视觉与触感。

paragraphs.en: By this point, I’d started to worry that new technology is pulling the creative act too far upstream from the final product, away from matters of execution. This is true for slop merchants, who write prompts and then spam the internet with the results regardless of their merit. It was also true for many—not all, but many—of the AI works in “Strange Rules,” which tended to make me think a lot about abstract ideas while feeling very little.
paragraphs.zh: 到这时，我开始担心新技术把创作行为从最终成品中推得太远，离执行层面太远。这对“垃圾作品商”来说确实如此——他们只是写好提示词，然后不论质量高低都向互联网铺天盖地地输出成果。它也同样适用于《怪异法则》里许多（不是全部，但不少）AI 作品，它们往往让我在阅读时思考大量抽象观念，却几乎没有情感触动。

paragraphs.en: Herndon at her desk in Berlin (Mattia Balsamini for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: 柏林办公室里专心工作中的 Herndon（Mattia Balsamini 摄，《大西洋月刊》）

paragraphs.en: A video installation by the French artist Fabien Giraud had a fabulously interesting backstory: AI-operated cameras, set up in a forest, were programmed to film whatever happened there for the next thousand years. The artist had staged a scene for those cameras to capture, featuring actors playing medieval hermits getting high on psychoactive fungus. But the footage shown in the palazzo was deadly boring.
paragraphs.zh: 法国艺术家法比安·吉罗（Fabien Giraud）的一件视频装置有着令人惊叹的背景故事：他在一片森林里架设了由人工智能操控的摄像机，编程为那里的景象持续拍摄未来一千年。艺术家为这些摄像机安排了一个场景，由演员扮演吸食致幻真菌的中世纪隐士来表演。可是在那座古典建筑中展出的影像却惊人地无聊。

paragraphs.en: Another artist, Trevor Paglen, had set up private listening booths for visitors to experience AI-assisted hypnosis—the latest effort in his career-long exploration of surveillance and psyops (including photographing secret military bases, such as Area 51). I lay back in a chaise lounge and put a biometric reader on my finger, nervous about what was going to happen. But the voice in my headphones just led me through a hokey guided meditation about meeting my “future self,” which was less entrancing than any given therapy app.
paragraphs.zh: 另一位艺术家 Trevor Paglen 为观众搭建了私人的聆听亭，让他们体验 AI 辅助下的催眠，这是他长期探索监控与心理战（psyops）创作历程中的最新尝试之一，包括拍摄秘密军事基地（例如51区）。我躺在长沙发式躺椅上，指尖戴上了生物识别读数器，心里有些紧张，不知道接下来会发生什么。结果耳机里的声音只是在引导我进行一段有些俗套的冥想，内容是与“未来的自己”相遇，这种体验比任何一个常见的疗愈应用都没那么入神。

paragraphs.en: These works were, alas, closer to janky science experiments than powerful aesthetic objects. AI can automate the craft that once went into creating images, stories, and songs; it can also enable mind-bending new projects. But there’s no shortcut to accomplishing the true task of art: bridging the creator’s subjectivity and the viewer’s, casting an emotional spell and communicating beyond language. When media are infinite, the details poured into any individual work matter more, not less.
paragraphs.zh: 遗憾的是，这些作品离强烈的审美对象还差得很远，更像是生硬的科学实验。AI 可以自动化过去用于创作图像、故事和歌曲的工艺，也能够催生令人意想不到的新项目。但艺术真正的任务并没有捷径：要搭起创作者主观经验与观众主观经验之间的桥梁，施展情感上的共鸣法则，并在语言之外进行沟通。当媒介变得无限时，投入到每一件单独作品中的细节反而更重要，而不是更不重要。

paragraphs.en: By the time I came back to Palazzo Diedo on my last full day in Venice, the screens had finally arrived. They were rather puny and slender—like something that displays your order at a drive-through—and they were showing a distorted Linux desktop. Dryhurst explained that the screens were half the size he’d ordered, and had malfunctioned due to what he suspected was tampering just a few hours after they were mounted. (“In my heart of hearts, I’d love for it to be some protest gesture,” he said. “It probably isn’t.”) He was considering taking the screens down entirely and letting the work exist as simply a sound installation.
paragraphs.zh: 当我在威尼斯度过最后一个整天并返回 Palazzo Diedo 时，那些屏幕终于到了。它们体积很小很纤细——像是显示外卖取餐信息的那种——画面里却是一个扭曲的 Linux 桌面。Dryhurst 解释说，这些屏幕只有他订购尺寸的一半，而且在安装后仅几个小时就出现故障，他怀疑是被人动过手脚导致的。（“说心里话，我很希望这真的是一种抗议手势，”他说，“但大概不会是。”）他正在考虑把屏幕完全取下，让作品只保留为一件声音装置。

paragraphs.en: With the palazzo largely empty of visitors that morning, I finally had time to closely contemplate Herndon and Dryhurst’s work. Synthetic sounds whizzed and ricocheted through the air, creating a strange yet satisfying counterpoint and harmony. Every so often, the weather in the room seemed to change: from tense discordance, to rhythmic intensity, to heavenly arpeggios. Herndon had spent the previous month figuring out, with Claude Code’s help, how to generate music based on the AIs’ “conversations.” It was challenging, she said, to give up the composer’s prerogative to “control time”: The agents decided what happened when, not her.
paragraphs.zh: 那天早上，宫殿里几乎没有访客，我终于有时间细看 Herndon 和 Dryhurst 的作品。合成音在空中呼啸、反弹，形成一种奇异却令人满足的对位与和声。时不时，房间里的“天气”似乎会变化：从紧张的失和，转为节奏的强度，再到天籁般的分解和弦。Herndon 在上个月和 Claude Code 一起研究，花了好一段时间才弄明白如何根据 AI 的“对话”来生成音乐。她说，最有挑战性的地方在于放弃作曲家的“掌控时间”的特权——不是她来决定发生什么，而是这些代理系统自己决定。

paragraphs.en: Even so, the results sounded—and felt—like her handiwork. Sitting on one of the benches, I couldn’t tell whether the AIs were wise or unwise, helpful consultants or tyrannical overlords. But the art hummed with the same sense of anticipation and curiosity I’d often felt when speaking with Herndon and Dryhurst. Visually, the installation was closer to a chapel than a parliament. But it didn’t feel like it existed for the worship of AI; rather, it conveyed belief in the prerogative to explore. I felt immersed in something larger than myself while also situated in an achingly specific time and place. I could have sat there for a long time.
paragraphs.zh: 尽管如此，结果听起来——也感觉起来——像是她的手艺。坐在其中一张长椅上，我分不清这些 AI 是明智还是愚昧，是乐于助人的顾问还是专横的统治者。不过，这件作品散发出的氛围，与我与赫尔农和德赖赫斯特交谈时常常体会到的那种期待与好奇完全一致。视觉上，这个装置更像一座小教堂，而不是议会。但它并不让人觉得是为了崇拜 AI 而存在；反而传达出一种对探索权利的信念。我感到自己沉浸在比自己更宏大的事物之中，同时又立足在一个令人心痛地具体的时空里。我本可以在那里坐上很久。

paragraphs.en: Herndon, Dryhurst, and I went out for coffee and sat on a small bridge over a canal. Tourists stepped over us to take selfies in front of the gondolas below. We got to talking about the challenges—logistical, budgetary, personal—that always come with doing ambitious work, no matter the technological era. Their exhibition had been planned and created with all sorts of AI tools, but as Herndon put it, “there’s a lot that you can’t AI your way out of.”
paragraphs.zh: 赫尔农、德赖赫斯特和我一起去喝咖啡，坐在一座横跨运河的小桥上。游客从我们身上跨过去，在下方的贡多拉前拍照打卡。我们开始谈起做有野心的创作永远会遇到的困难——无论处在什么技术时代，都是后勤、预算和个人层面的挑战。虽然他们的展览是用各种 AI 工具策划并完成的，但正如赫尔农所说，“总有很多东西，是你无法用 AI 解决的。”

paragraphs.en: One “Strange Rules” work, by Simon Denny and Venkatesh Rao, was titled Monsters Between Worlds—a reference to a quotation often attributed to Antonio Gramsci: “The old world is dying, and the new world struggles to be born: Now is the time of monsters.” That gap, Dryhurst said, is “being populated by scammers and idealists and utopians and really good people filling a vacuum left by a failing story of the 20th century that needs to be updated.” But I’d come to suspect that AI will neither redeem nor destroy the old world, just spackle another layer of imperfect creation upon it—and maybe soon, with enough human care, a bit of greatness.
paragraphs.zh: 《陌生规则》中的一件作品，由西蒙·丹尼和温卡特什·拉奥合作完成，题为《世界之间的怪物》；它引自常被归于安东尼奥·葛兰西的一句话：“旧世界正在消逝，而新世界在努力诞生：这是怪物的时代。”德赖赫斯特说，这个间隙正“被骗子、理想主义者、乌托邦主义者以及许多非常好的人占据，他们在填补一个过时的、正在失效并需要更新的20世纪叙事所留下的真空。”但我越来越确信，AI既不会拯救旧世界，也不会摧毁它，而是会在其上再抹上一层不完美的创造；也许不久之后，在足够多的人类呵护下，还会出现一点伟大。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the August 2026 print edition with the headline “What AI Will Do to Art.”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章刊登于2026年8月纸质版，标题是“人工智能将对艺术做什么”。
